description: Review key enzyme concepts with these flashcards (BI2.1-5).

cards.text: What is the difference between an apoenzyme and a holoenzyme?
cards.answer: Apoenzyme is the protein part alone (inactive)/Apoenzyme plus cofactor equals holoenzyme (active)

cards.text: What does Km represent in Michaelis-Menten kinetics?
cards.answer: The substrate concentration at which reaction velocity is half of Vmax/It reflects enzyme-substrate affinity — low Km means high affinity

tip.tip: Km is inversely related to affinity

cards.text: How does a competitive inhibitor affect Km and Vmax?
cards.answer: Increases apparent Km (lower affinity) but Vmax remains unchanged/Can be overcome by increasing substrate concentration

cards.text: Name the six IUBMB enzyme classes.
cards.answer: Oxidoreductases, Transferases, Hydrolases, Lyases, Isomerases, Ligases/Mnemonic: Over The Hill Lies Igloo Land

tip.tip: Classification is based on the type of reaction catalysed

cards.text: Why is ALT more specific for liver injury than AST?
cards.answer: ALT is found almost exclusively in hepatocyte cytoplasm/AST is present in liver, heart, skeletal muscle, and kidney

cards.text: What is an isoenzyme? Give a clinical example.
cards.answer: Different molecular forms of the same enzyme catalysing the same reaction/LDH has 5 isoforms — LDH1 predominates in heart, LDH5 in liver

cards.text: How do non-competitive inhibitors differ from competitive inhibitors?
cards.answer: Non-competitive inhibitors bind at an allosteric site, decrease Vmax, and do not change Km/Cannot be overcome by adding more substrate

tip.tip: They change enzyme shape, not block substrate binding

cards.text: What is the clinical significance of elevated serum amylase and lipase?
cards.answer: Both are elevated in acute pancreatitis/Lipase is more specific and stays elevated longer than amylase

tip.tip: Think: pancreatic enzymes leak into blood when pancreas is inflamed

cards.text: What is a coenzyme? Name two examples derived from vitamins.
cards.answer: An organic non-protein molecule that assists enzyme catalysis/NAD+ from niacin (vitamin B3) and FAD from riboflavin (vitamin B2)

tip.tip: Most coenzymes are derived from water-soluble B vitamins

cards.answer: Aspirin irreversibly acetylates a serine residue in the active site of COX/This permanently inactivates the enzyme, blocking prostaglandin synthesis

tip.tip: Irreversible inhibition — enzyme must be resynthesised
